text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Helen Morris  
Riverside Medical Practice  
14 Mill Street, London SE1 3QR  

Dear Dr Helen Morris,  

Alex Turner, DOB: 22-08-1988, Hospital No: U914725 NHS No. 3849201756  
Flat 3, 29 King’s Wharf, London, SE8 5JP 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Focal epilepsy secondary to left temporal cavernoma; migraine without aura; generalised anxiety disorder; vitamin D insufficiency; former smoker; lives independently with community support as needed. 

Medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily.  
Lacosamide 100 mg twice daily.  
Sertraline 50 mg once daily.  
Cholecalciferol 1000 IU once daily.  
Sumatriptan 50 mg as needed for migraine (max 2/day).  

I reviewed Alex in clinic today following several recent emergency department (ED) attendances over the summer. Multiple recent ED triage records exist; nursing documentation summarised where relevant. The ED notes from 12 June, 28 July, and 05 September document brief right-hand and facial jerks with preserved awareness, lasting under a minute, consistent with his known focal motor seizures. Nursing notes describe rapid recovery, no post-ictal confusion, normal observations, and no injuries. ED teams did not administer rescue medication. 

Seizures: Alex keeps a digital diary which I reconciled with the ED timestamps. Over the most recent quarter, the pattern is of brief myoclonic-type focal motor jerks localising to the right face/hand with intact awareness. In his words, frequency has ranged from “4 to 22 myoclonic in three months,” reflecting clustering during periods of sleep deprivation and stress. He denies any loss of consciousness or secondary generalisation during this interval. No clear triggers other than poor sleep and missed meals. 

Background epilepsy history: Focal epilepsy was first diagnosed in 2019 following two stereotyped events and MRI evidence of a left mesial temporal cavernoma. Neuropsychology in 2020 showed mild verbal memory weakness. EEG in 2019 demonstrated left temporal interictal spikes. He has responded partially to lamotrigine; lacosamide was added in 2023 for breakthrough focal motor events. He has not had a generalised tonic-clonic seizure since 2020. 

Recent interval history: Since our last clinic in March 2025, Alex reports increased work-related stress, variable sleep, and two missed evening doses of lamotrigine in August. He denies alcohol or recreational drug use. Appetite is fair; weight stable. Migraine frequency is approximately 1–2 per month, responding to sumatriptan. No new neurological deficits. 

Examination: Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Motor: normal bulk and tone, full power throughout, no tremor. Sensation intact. Coordination and gait normal. No tongue biting or injuries. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. 

Investigations: 
- ED capillary glucose and electrolytes on 12/06, 28/07, and 05/09 were within reference ranges; no metabolic precipitants identified.  
- Serum lamotrigine level (drawn 06/09, morning trough) 3.7 mg/L (laboratory reference range 3–14).  
- Vitamin D in March 2025 was 24 nmol/L; on replacement since. Repeat pending.  
- MRI brain (2023) unchanged left mesial temporal cavernoma; no new lesions. 

Assessment: Focal epilepsy with brief focal motor seizures involving right face/hand, temporally linked to sleep deprivation and occasional missed doses. The reported range “4 to 22 myoclonic in three months” aligns with the ED corroboration and nursing documentation, suggesting fluctuating seizure burden rather than progression to generalised events. No red flags on examination. Subtherapeutic lamotrigine trough likely contributed. 

Plan: 
- Increase lamotrigine to 175 mg twice daily for two weeks, then 200 mg twice daily if tolerated; monitor for rash or dizziness.  
- Continue lacosamide 100 mg twice daily.  
- Bloods at GP in 4 weeks: FBC, U&E, LFT, lamotrigine level (trough), vitamin D.  
- Sleep hygiene and regular meals reinforced; provided written guidance.  
- Rescue plan: If clustering exceeds baseline or any loss of awareness occurs, attend ED and inform our epilepsy service.  
- DVLA: Patient is not driving and understands need to inform DVLA if awareness-impairing events occur.  
- Follow-up in epilepsy clinic in 3 months, sooner if deterioration.  

Yours sincerely

entities.text: frequency has ranged from “4 to 22 myoclonic in three months,”

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: “frequency has ranged from ‘4 to 22 myoclonic in three months,’” referring to Alex’s focal motor seizures over the most recent quarter. We must select the highest frequency when multiple are presented. Therefore, use the upper bound 22 over 3 months. Convert to allowed format: '22 per 3 month'.
seizure_frequency_number: frequency has ranged from “4 to 22 myoclonic in three months,”
